children.name: Haematopus
children.description: Noisy pied waders of rocky shores. The bright orange bill is used to prise open mussels or stab open cockles — each individual learns one technique and uses it for life. Their loud piping calls are a defining sound of the Swedish archipelago coastline.

children.name: Haematopus ostralegus
children.description: The Eurasian oystercatcher or European oystercatcher, also known as the common pied oystercatcher, or just oystercatcher, is a wader in the oystercatcher bird family Haematopodidae. It has striking black and white plumage, a long straight orange-red bill, red eyes and relatively short dull pink legs. The sexes are similar in appearance but the bill of the female is longer than that of the male.

children.name: Haematopus palliatus
children.description: A large pied oystercatcher of American Atlantic and Pacific coasts with a brown back and bright orange-red bill. Feeds on bivalves and marine worms on sandy and shell beaches. IUCN Least Concern but locally sensitive to coastal disturbance.

children.name: Haematopus bachmani
children.description: An all-black oystercatcher of rocky Pacific coasts from Alaska to Baja California. A keystone indicator of intertidal health. IUCN Least Concern but a species of high conservation concern due to small population.

children.name: Haematopus moquini
children.description: An entirely black oystercatcher endemic to the coasts of southern Africa. Once Near Threatened, its numbers recovered following protection and mussel colonisation, now IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Haematopus fuliginosus
children.description: A wholly black oystercatcher of Australian rocky and sandy shores with a striking red eye-ring and bill. Strictly coastal, rarely venturing inland. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Haematopus longirostris
children.description: A black-and-white oystercatcher of Australian and New Guinean beaches and estuaries. Prefers sandy ocean shores over rocky reefs. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Haematopus unicolor
children.description: The variable oystercatcher is a species of wader in the family Haematopodidae. It is endemic to New Zealand. The Māori name is tōrea-pango.

children.name: Haematopus finschi
children.description: The South Island oystercatcher or South Island pied oystercatcher is one of two common oystercatcher species found in New Zealand. Its name is often contracted to the acronym "SIPO". The indigenous Māori name is tōrea.

children.name: Haematopus chathamensis
children.description: A rare oystercatcher restricted to the Chatham Islands of New Zealand, with a population of only a few hundred. IUCN Endangered, the subject of intensive nest-protection programs against introduced predators.

children.name: Haematopus ater
children.description: A sooty-black oystercatcher of the rocky coasts of southern South America, from Peru and Brazil to Tierra del Fuego. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Haematopus leucopodus
children.description: A pied oystercatcher of southern South America and the Falkland Islands, with a bold yellow eye-ring. Breeds on inland grassland and bogs as well as the coast. IUCN Least Concern.

children.name: Haematopus meadewaldoi
children.description: An all-black oystercatcher formerly endemic to the eastern Canary Islands, last reliably recorded around 1940 and now considered Extinct, likely due to overharvesting of its intertidal invertebrate prey.

children.name: Haematopus ophthalmicus
children.description: The eastern far-eastern populations of the oystercatcher complex breeding in Kamchatka and northeast Asia, sometimes treated as a distinct race, with a very prominent orange eye-ring. Coastal and estuarine.
